common_name: Flamingo Flower (Pigtail Plant, Flamingo Lily, Scarlet Flamingo)

planting.note: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, chunky aroid mix; Anthurium scherzerianum is a compact, free-flowering anthurium from the cloud forests of Costa Rica and Guatemala that produces waxy, brilliantly colored spathes in red, orange, or pink throughout the year, paired with a distinctively coiled, corkscrew-spiral orange spadix — the 'pigtail' that distinguishes it from the flat-spadixed A. andraeanum. Very compact (12–18 inches), it is one of the best anthuriums for shaded patio containers in zone 10-12. Water moderately, allowing slight drying between watering. CAUTION: toxic to cats and dogs.

directions: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained, chunky aroid mix — Flamingo Flower is one of the most free-flowering and adaptable of the anthurium species for shaded indoor and outdoor ornamental use, producing its brilliantly colored waxy spathes through most of the year in warm, bright-shaded conditions. Anthurium scherzerianum is a compact, clumping, epiphytic anthurium in the Araceae family native to the cloud forests of Costa Rica and Guatemala, and the smaller of the two most commonly cultivated ornamental anthurium species (the other being the larger, flat-spadixed A. andraeanum). The plant is immediately distinguished by its distinctive inflorescence: a waxy, shield-shaped spathe 2–3 inches across in vivid scarlet-red (also available in pink, salmon, orange, and white forms) paired with a uniquely coiled, spiral, corkscrew-curled spadix in orange-yellow — the 'pigtail' that gives the plant one of its common names and distinguishes it from all other commonly grown anthuriums. The compact size (12–18 inches in height and spread), continuous blooming habit, and tolerance of lower light levels make A. scherzerianum one of the ideal anthurium species for shaded patio containers, interior plants, and protected outdoor positions in zone 10-12. The waxy spathes are extremely long-lasting — individual inflorescences remain fresh and ornamental for 6–10 weeks on the plant. Produces flowers continuously in warm conditions, with multiple spathes at different stages of development simultaneously. CAUTION: toxic to pets.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Anthurium scherzerianum for Florida shade gardens
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium scherzerianum species profile
American Horticultural Society — Anthurium scherzerianum: Flamingo Flower cultivation
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to cats and dogs)

common_name: Fingers Anthurium (Cut-Leaf Anthurium, Radiating Finger Anthurium, Mexican Finger Anthurium)

planting.note: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, chunky aroid mix; Anthurium pedatoradiatum produces leaves that are deeply and narrowly divided into 9–11 fingerlike segments radiating from a central point — an unusual, dramatically different leaf form from the typical heart-shaped anthurium leaf. The overall leaf can reach 18–24 inches across in mature plants, making it a bold architectural statement in shaded spaces. More tolerant of intermediate conditions than many velvet anthuriums. Keep warm (above 55°F) and moderately moist. CAUTION: toxic to cats and dogs.

directions: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil — Fingers Anthurium is one of the most structurally unusual of the ornamental anthuriums, producing leaves that are dramatically divided into narrow, spreading, fingerlike segments rather than the entire (undivided) heart-shaped or arrow-shaped form typical of the genus. Anthurium pedatoradiatum is a climbing, hemiepiphytic anthurium in the Araceae family native to southern Mexico (Chiapas, Tabasco, Veracruz) and Central America, one of a handful of Anthurium species that produce leaves divided into multiple narrow lobes or segments (a condition called pedatifid or palmatifid division). The plant produces leaves that are deeply divided into 9–11 narrow, lanceolate to oblong segments (lobes) radiating from a central point in the pattern for which the species name pedatoradiatum ('radiating fingers') was given — the individual segments smooth, dark green, and spreading outward from the center like the fingers of an outspread hand. In juvenile plants, the leaves begin simple and heart-shaped, transitioning to the deeply divided adult form as the plant matures and climbs. Fully adult leaves in well-established plants can reach 18–24 inches across, producing a dramatic, palm-like or philodendron-selloum-like silhouette in a shaded garden. The unusual leaf division makes this one of the most architecturally distinctive anthuriums for interior and shaded tropical garden design. CAUTION: toxic to pets.
sources: International Aroid Society — Anthurium pedatoradiatum cultivation notes
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium pedatoradiatum species profile
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Unusual Anthurium species for Florida collections
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to cats and dogs)

common_name: Moonlight Scindapsus (Treub's Scindapsus, Dark Form Scindapsus, Satin Pothos Relative)

planting.note: Grow in partial to deep shade in well-drained, moist, chunky aroid mix with a moss pole, bark board, or rough surface to climb; Scindapsus treubii has two widely grown forms — 'Moonlight' (silver-green leaves with strong silver-white overlay) and 'Dark Form' (very deep, almost black-green leaves with subtle steel-blue iridescence). Both forms are highly sought collector aroids. The plant is closely related to Scindapsus pictus (Satin Pothos) but produces narrower, more elongated, distinctly different leaves. Keep warm (above 60°F) and provide consistent humidity. CAUTION: toxic to cats and dogs.

directions: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained, well-aerated aroid mix with a rough support to climb — Moonlight Scindapsus produces its most ornamental foliage in bright indirect to low light conditions and is an outstanding climbing shade vine for shaded indoor and outdoor tropical positions. Scindapsus treubii is a climbing, hemiepiphytic perennial aroid in the Araceae family native to Borneo, Java, and Southeast Asia — a member of the genus Scindapsus, which is closely related to but distinct from both Epipremnum (pothos) and Monstera, in the tribe Monstereae. The plant produces elongated, narrowly ovate leaves (4–6 inches long, distinctly narrow compared to S. pictus) on climbing stems that ascend with adhesive aerial roots. Two distinct leaf color forms have become highly popular in the collector aroid market: the 'Moonlight' form, which produces silver-green to pale sage-green leaves with a strong silver overlay that gives each leaf a luminous, silver-washed quality — and the 'Dark Form,' which produces leaves of a very deep, saturated dark forest-green to near-black, with a subtle, gunmetal blue-green iridescence in certain light. Both forms are striking and distinctly different from any common houseplant. The plant climbs readily when given a rough moss pole or bark board and develops larger, more lustrous leaves as it ascends. Moderately fast-growing in warm conditions. One of the most sought-after collector aroids of the past decade. CAUTION: toxic to pets.
sources: International Aroid Society — Scindapsus treubii: Moonlight and Dark Form cultivation
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Scindapsus treubii species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Collector aroids for Florida tropical gardens
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Scindapsus (toxic to cats and dogs)

common_name: Dumbcane (Leopard Lily, Tropic Snow Dieffenbachia, Giant Dumbcane)

planting.note: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil or container mix; Dieffenbachia amoena is one of the largest Dieffenbachia species, producing bold canes 4–6 feet tall with large (18–24 inch) leaves in deep green with irregular cream to white markings along the midrib and veins. One of the most tolerant large shade plants for low-light indoor and outdoor shaded positions. Water moderately, allowing the top inch of soil to dry between waterings. CAUTION: all Dieffenbachia parts are toxic if ingested — the calcium oxalate crystals and protein-digesting enzymes cause temporary but severe swelling of the mouth and throat ('dumb,' unable to speak, hence the common name). Keep away from children and pets.

directions: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained soil — Dumbcane is one of the most shade-tolerant, bold-textured, large-leaved ornamental plants for low-light interior and shaded outdoor positions, and one of the most widely grown tropical ornamentals in the world for this reason. Dieffenbachia amoena is a large, upright, cane-forming perennial aroid in the Araceae family native to tropical America (Colombia, Brazil, Central America) — one of the larger and more robust species in the genus Dieffenbachia, which comprises approximately 60 species of tropical aroids grown widely as ornamental foliage plants throughout the world. The plant produces thick, semi-woody canes 4–6 feet tall clothed in large, broadly elliptic to ovate leaves 18–24 inches long. The leaves are the ornamental feature: deep, lustrous, forest-green with irregular stripes, blotches, and feathering of cream to white along and between the primary veins — creating a boldly variegated pattern of dark and light across the large leaf surface. The plant is extraordinarily tolerant of low light, irregular watering, and indoor conditions, making it one of the classic large interior foliage plants of offices, lobbies, and shaded indoor spaces worldwide, and a permanent outdoor specimen in zone 10-12 landscapes. The common name 'Dumbcane' refers to the toxic effects of chewing on the plant: the calcium oxalate crystals and proteolytic enzymes cause immediate, severe swelling of the mouth and throat that temporarily renders the victim unable to speak. CAUTION: severely toxic if ingested — keep away from children and pets.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dieffenbachia species for Florida shade gardens
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Dieffenbachia amoena species profile
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Dieffenbachia (toxic to cats, dogs, and horses)
Poison Control Center — Dieffenbachia: calcium oxalate toxicity

common_name: Dragon Taro (Many-Leaved Dracontium, Dragon Arum, Giant Dragon Lily)

planting.note: Plant corm 4–6 inches deep in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, very well-drained, fertile soil; Dracontium polyphyllum is one of the most dramatically exotic-looking of the large tropical aroids, producing a single compound leaf on a single, spectacularly mottled, snake-skin-patterned petiole — the entire leaf can reach 4–6 feet across in mature specimens and is divided into dozens of leaflets arranged like a giant tropical fern frond. The mottled, green-and-brown, snake-skin-patterned petiole 3–5 feet tall is particularly dramatic. The corm blooms underground or near soil level on a short spadix before the leaf emerges, with a strong, fetid odor for 1–2 days. Dormant in winter. CAUTION: toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant in partial shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, fertile soil — Dragon Taro grows most spectacularly in the warm, moist, deeply shaded conditions of a sheltered tropical garden and produces one of the most dramatically exotic single-leaf structures of any ornamental plant when mature. Dracontium polyphyllum is a large, corm-based, deciduous perennial aroid in the Araceae family native to tropical South America (Colombia, Venezuela, Brazil, Peru, Ecuador) — one of approximately 22 species in the genus Dracontium, a group of large, highly ornamental aroids closely related to Amorphophallus. Like the related Amorphophallus, each Dracontium plant consists of a single, compound leaf emerging annually from an underground corm, preceded by an often briefly-fetid inflorescence at or near the soil surface. The leaf is the great spectacle: in Dracontium polyphyllum, it consists of a single, erect petiole (leaf stalk) 3–5 feet tall — dramatically patterned in irregular blotches and spots of brown, green, and gray, like a snake or dragon skin — topped by a massive, spreading, compound blade that divides repeatedly into dozens of individual leaflets arranged in a broad, flat, fern-frond or umbrella-like canopy reaching 4–6 feet across. The overall visual impression of a mature Dragon Taro leaf is extraordinary — an otherworldly, tropical umbrella combining exotic patterning and massive scale. In zone 10-12, the corm overwinters in the ground and produces a new leaf each spring. CAUTION: irritating if ingested raw.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Dracontium polyphyllum species profile
International Aroid Society — Dracontium polyphyllum cultivation notes
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Rare tropical aroids for Florida collections
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Dracontium (irritant if ingested)
